tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Atlahua is a lesser-known Aztec god associated with water, fishing, and hunting.
  • He was worshipped by communities that depended on lakes and rivers.
  • He represents survival, food gathering, and natural balance.
  • He protects fishermen and ensures successful hunts.
  • He symbolizes the connection between humans and aquatic ecosystems.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Water Control – Influences lakes and rivers.
  • Hunting Skill – Grants precision and success.
  • Survival Instinct – Enhances human endurance.
  • Animal Awareness – Connects with aquatic life.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content:
• Mainly worshipped near water bodies.
  • Represents daily survival rather than cosmic power.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Ayauhteotl is the goddess of mist, fog, and atmospheric mystery.
  • She is associated with the unseen and hidden aspects of nature.
  • She represents unpredictability and illusion.
  • She is linked to rain and transformation.
  • Her presence symbolizes the unknown forces of nature.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Mist Control – Creates dense fog.
  • Illusion – Obscures vision.
  • Rain Influence – Supports weather cycles.
  • Stealth – Hides presence.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content:
• Often associated with mysterious weather changes.
  • Represents hidden forces of nature.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Chalchiuhtlicue is the goddess of rivers, lakes, and oceans.
  • She is associated with fertility and childbirth.
  • She is considered the partner of Tlaloc.
  • She nurtures life through water.
  • She represents purity and renewal.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Water Control – Governs aquatic systems.
  • Fertility – Supports life growth.
  • Healing – Symbolizes cleansing.
  • Protection – Guards travelers.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content:
• Her name means “Jade Skirt”.
  • Important in water rituals.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Chicomecoatl is the goddess of food and nourishment.
  • She ensures survival through agriculture.
  • She is linked to maize and harvest.
  • She represents abundance and prosperity.
  • She plays a key role in sustaining human life.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Food Creation – Provides nourishment.
  • Fertility – Enhances crops.
  • Harvest Blessing – Ensures abundance.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Centeotl is the god of maize, the staple crop of the Aztecs.
  • He represents food, growth, and survival.
  • He ensures agricultural success.
  • He symbolizes nourishment.
  • He is central to Aztec farming culture.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Growth – Enhances crops.
  • Fertility – Supports agriculture.
  • Harvest Control – Ensures yield.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Citlalatonac is associated with stars and cosmic creation.
  • He represents celestial energy.
  • He is part of cosmic duality.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Citlalicue is the goddess of the stars and heavens.
  • She represents the night sky.
  • She symbolizes cosmic beauty.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Coatlicue is the mother goddess of earth and life.
  • She represents both creation and destruction.
  • She is the mother of many gods.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Coyolxauhqui is the goddess of the moon.
  • She represents night and cycles.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Ehecatl is the wind god and an aspect of Quetzalcoatl.
  • He brings wind that helps rain fall.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Huehueteotl is the ancient god of fire and one of the oldest deities in Aztec mythology.
  • He represents warmth, life energy, and transformation.
  • He is associated with the hearth and domestic life.
  • Fire was seen as both creative and destructive through him.
  • He symbolizes renewal and the cycle of life.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Fire Control – Manipulates flames and heat.
  • Renewal – Represents rebirth through fire.
  • Energy Source – Sustains life through warmth.
  • Destruction – Can burn and destroy.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content:
• Often depicted as an old man.
  • Linked with Xiuhtecuhtli.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Huitzilopochtli is the central war god of the Aztecs and patron of Tenochtitlan.
  • He represents the sun and the power of warfare.
  • He guided the Aztecs to build their capital.
  • He symbolizes strength, victory, and dominance.
  • He required sacrifices to maintain cosmic balance.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• War Mastery – Supreme battle power.
  • Solar Energy – Controls the sun.
  • Strength – Immense physical power.
  • Leadership – Commands armies.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content:
• One of the most important Aztec gods.
  • Symbol of Aztec military strength.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Itzpapalotl is a fearsome goddess associated with night and death.
  • She is depicted as a skeletal butterfly with obsidian blades.
  • She represents darkness and transformation.
  • She rules over certain celestial realms.
  • She symbolizes both destruction and rebirth.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Dark Energy – Controls night forces.
  • Transformation – Changes form.
  • Magic – Uses supernatural abilities.
  • Destruction – Associated with death.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Ixtlilton is the god of healing, medicine, and health.
  • He helps cure diseases and maintain well-being.
  • He is associated with peace and recovery.
  • He represents calmness and care.
  • He is worshipped for protection from illness.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Healing – Cures illness.
  • Medicine – Supports health.
  • Protection – Guards against disease.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Macuilxochitl is the god of pleasure, music, and games.
  • He represents joy and entertainment.
  • He is linked with gambling and celebrations.
  • He symbolizes both fun and risk.
  • He is associated with artistic expression.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Joy – Brings happiness.
  • Music – Inspires art.
  • Luck – Influences games.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Metztli is the god of the moon.
  • He represents night cycles.
  • He influences tides and time.
  • He symbolizes calmness.
  • He is part of cosmic balance.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Mictlantecuhtli is the ruler of the underworld.
  • He governs the land of the dead.
  • He represents death and darkness.
  • He is a powerful and feared deity.
  • He symbolizes the end of life.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Mictecacihuatl is the queen of the underworld.
  • She rules alongside Mictlantecuhtli.
  • She oversees the souls of the dead.
  • She represents death and remembrance.
  • She is associated with festivals of the dead.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content: • Linked to Day of the Dead traditions.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Mixcoatl is the god of hunting and stars.
  • He represents skill and survival.
  • He is associated with warriors.
  • He symbolizes strength and precision.
  • He connects humans with the cosmos.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Ometeotl is the dual creator deity representing male and female aspects.
  • He embodies balance and unity.
  • He is the source of all creation.
  • He represents cosmic harmony.
  • He exists beyond normal gods.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content: • Male aspect of Ometeotl.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content: • Female aspect of Ometeotl.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Quetzalcoatl is one of the most important Aztec gods.
  • He represents knowledge, wind, and creation.
  • He is associated with civilization and learning.
  • He symbolizes wisdom and order.
  • He is a creator deity.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Tezcatlipoca is one of the most powerful Aztec gods, representing night, fate, and conflict.
  • He is a rival of Quetzalcoatl and symbolizes chaos and change.
  • He is associated with kingship and power.
  • He influences destiny and human actions.
  • He represents unpredictability and strength.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Illusion – Manipulates reality.
  • Magic – Uses supernatural abilities.
  • War – Controls conflict and battle.
  • Destiny – Influences fate.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content:
• His name means “Smoking Mirror”.
  • Often depicted with a mirror on his chest.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Teteoinnan is a mother goddess associated with healing and fertility.
  • She represents nurturing and protection.
  • She is connected with medicine and herbs.
  • She symbolizes motherhood and care.
  • She is deeply respected by healers.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Healing – Restores health.
  • Fertility – Supports life growth.
  • Protection – Guards families.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Tlaloc is the powerful god of rain, storms, and agriculture.
  • He controls rainfall essential for crops.
  • He can bring both life-giving rain and destructive storms.
  • He represents nature’s dual power.
  • He is one of the most important Aztec gods.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Rain Control – Governs rainfall.
  • Storm Creation – Creates lightning and storms.
  • Fertility – Supports agriculture.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content: • Children were sacrificed to him in rituals.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Tlazolteotl is the goddess of purification and forgiveness.
  • She absorbs sins and cleanses people.
  • She represents both vice and redemption.
  • She symbolizes transformation and renewal.
  • She plays a role in moral balance.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Tonantzin is a mother goddess representing earth and fertility.
  • She nurtures life and protects people.
  • She symbolizes motherhood and care.
  • She is deeply respected in Aztec culture.
  • She represents nourishment and life.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Tonatiuh is the sun god and central figure in Aztec cosmology.
  • He represents life energy and strength.
  • He demands sacrifices to continue shining.
  • He symbolizes power and survival.
  • He is essential for life on Earth.

tab2.title: Powers
tab2.content:
• Solar Energy – Controls the sun.
  • Life Force – Sustains existence.

tab3.title: Facts
tab3.content: • Central figure in the Aztec calendar stone.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Xipe Totec is the god of renewal and agriculture.
  • He represents rebirth through shedding skin.
  • He symbolizes transformation.
  • He is associated with crops and growth.
  • He represents new beginnings.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Xiuhtecuhtli is the god of fire and time.
  • He represents life energy.
  • He is central to rituals.
  • He symbolizes continuity.
  • He is one of the oldest gods.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Xilonen is the goddess of young maize.
  • She represents growth and nourishment.
  • She symbolizes fertility.
  • She supports agriculture.
  • She represents life cycles.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Xochipilli is the god of art, music, and beauty.
  • He represents creativity.
  • He symbolizes joy and expression.
  • He is associated with flowers.
  • He inspires artists.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Xochiquetzal is the goddess of love and beauty.
  • She represents attraction.
  • She symbolizes fertility.
  • She is associated with flowers.
  • She represents desire.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Xolotl is the god of death and transformation.
  • He guides souls.
  • He represents change.
  • He is linked to lightning.
  • He symbolizes duality.

tab1.title: Overview
tab1.content:
• Yacatecuhtli is the god of merchants and travelers.
  • He protects journeys.
  • He represents trade and movement.
  • He guides people safely.
  • He symbolizes direction and purpose.
